title: To Wordsworth.
content:
Poet of Nature, thou hast wept to know
    That things depart which never may return:
    Childhood and youth, friendship and love's first glow,
    Have fled like sweet dreams, leaving thee to mourn.
    These common woes I feel. One loss is mine
    Which thou too feel'st, yet I alone deplore.
    Thou wert as a lone star, whose light did shine
    On some frail bark in winter's midnight roar:
    Thou hast like to a rock-built refuge stood
    Above the blind and battling multitude:
    In honoured poverty thy voice did weave
    Songs consecrate to truth and liberty, -
    Deserting these, thou leavest me to grieve,
    Thus having been, that thou shouldst cease to be.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Percy Bysshe Shelley, titled "To Wordsworth.", represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
